historical_cases.name: Post-9/11 U.S. Defense Buildup (2001–2003)
historical_cases.peak_move: Defense contractors (Lockheed, Raytheon, General Dynamics) observed cumulative gains of 30–60% over the buildup phase as Pentagon budgets surged and major platform contracts were awarded
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Initial Iraqi campaign declared complete (May 2003); defense stocks plateaued as investor focus shifted from contract-award anticipation to execution risk and budget sustainability debates

historical_cases.name: Post-Crimea NATO Rearmament Wave (2014–2016)
historical_cases.peak_move: European and U.S. defense equities saw modest but sustained re-rating; NATO members began announcing multi-year spending pledges toward the 2% GDP target, supporting sector outperformance
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Immediate geopolitical tension stabilized into frozen conflict; European fiscal constraints dampened follow-through spending, and investor attention rotated to other themes

historical_cases.name: Russia-Ukraine War Defense Surge (2022–2023)
historical_cases.peak_move: Global defense indices observed 30–50% sector outperformance in the 12 months following February 2022; European defense names (Rheinmetall, BAE Systems, Saab) saw particularly sharp re-ratings as continental rearmament became policy consensus
historical_cases.exit_trigger: War transitioned to attritional phase; near-term contract-award news flow moderated; broader market rotation into AI/tech absorbed investor capital; some producers flagged production bottlenecks limiting near-term revenue conversion

historical_cases.name: Reagan Defense Buildup (1981–1985)
historical_cases.peak_move: Defense sector sustained multi-year outperformance as Pentagon budgets nearly doubled in real terms; major platform programs (B-1B, MX missile, SDI) generated long-duration contract backlogs
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Gramm-Rudman deficit reduction legislation (1985–1986) introduced mandatory spending caps; the Iran-Contra affair created political headwinds; sector valuations had meaningfully re-rated, reducing further upside optionality

differences.factor: Simultaneous multi-theater demand across NATO eastern flank, Indo-Pacific deterrence, and Middle East replenishment creates a demand stack with no close post-WWII analog; historically, major buildups were concentrated in one primary theater

Defense industrial base faces structural production-rate ceilings — artillery shell capacity, shipyard throughput, and munitions supply chains were allowed to atrophy for decades; supply elasticity is materially lower than in prior buildups, meaning backlogs persist longer before revenue fully converts

European sovereign governments have made legally binding multi-year NATO spending commitments and in some cases (Germany's Sondervermögen €100B+ fund) created dedicated off-balance-sheet vehicles, reducing the political-cycle reversal risk that curtailed prior European rearmament waves

Drone warfare, electronic warfare, AI-assisted targeting, and hypersonic development represent a technology generational shift requiring entirely new platform categories and procurement programs — not merely more of existing systems — potentially sustaining a longer R&D-to-contract pipeline than historical analog buildups

similarities: As observed in all historical cases, initial sentiment-driven equity re-rating tends to outpace actual contract revenue realization by 12–24 months, creating vulnerability to 'show me the backlog conversion' corrections

Political consensus that initiates buildups has historically proven fragile over 3–5 year horizons as fiscal costs accumulate and domestic spending priorities compete — this dynamic may reassert itself in the current cycle
Supply chain and labor constraints delayed production ramp in prior buildups (notably post-9/11), and similar friction appears to be emerging in current munitions and shipbuilding capacity
this_time_different.observation: The current defense buildup cycle may structurally resemble a compressed version of the Reagan-era multi-year expansion more than the sharper post-9/11 or post-Crimea episodes, given the breadth of committed sovereign demand, but supply-side production ceilings and technology transition complexity introduce meaningful uncertainty about the pace at which backlog converts to earnings.

exit_signals: Major ceasefire or durable peace framework in the Russia-Ukraine conflict, materially reducing the urgency narrative driving European rearmament pledges and near-term procurement acceleration
Congressional or European parliamentary budget actions that introduce multi-year caps or rescissions to defense appropriations, signaling political appetite for spending has peaked

Defense sector forward P/E multiples expanding to historically extreme levels (observed in prior cycles as a precursor to mean reversion), reflecting full pricing of the backlog narrative without incremental contract catalysts

Key prime contractors (Lockheed, Rheinmetall, RTX, BAE) begin issuing production guidance downgrades or delivery schedule slippage warnings, indicating supply-side bottlenecks are constraining revenue conversion from record backlogs

Rotation of major institutional flows out of defense into competing structural themes (e.g., AI infrastructure, energy transition) as measured by sustained sector relative underperformance despite continued macro tailwinds
duration_type_reasoning: Defense spending surges are fundamentally dependent on the persistence of the geopolitical threat environment that justified them — historically, when the triggering conflict stabilizes or political consensus fractures, spending growth decelerates regardless of industrial base momentum.

real_world_timeline.description: The current defense buildup cycle originated with Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, which catalyzed a step-change in NATO member spending commitments, European rearmament programs, and U.S. supplemental defense appropriations that have continued through 2025–2026.
